bio.bio_blurb: Navy widow who settled in Dresden

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed, she sailed for Germany and stayed
narrative_blocks.body: When her husband, a Navy commander, died in 1882, her trips abroad grew longer and longer until in 1900 she simply stayed — settling in Dresden, where she died six years later. A nephew who traveled with her was said to be like the son she never had.

bio.bio_blurb: Navy widow who settled in Dresden

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed, she sailed for Germany and stayed
narrative_blocks.body: When her husband, a Navy commander, died in 1882, her trips abroad grew longer and longer until in 1900 she simply stayed — settling in Dresden, where she died six years later. A nephew who traveled with her was said to be like the son she never had.

notable.notable_blurb: US Navy Commander; Civil War; Naval Academy Commandant

narrative_blocks.header: Fought at Mobile Bay, New Orleans, and Vicksburg
narrative_blocks.body: As a junior officer aboard the steam sloop USS Richmond, he served throughout the Civil War with the Western Gulf Blockading Squadron. He fought in the engagement with the CSS Manassas, the capture of New Orleans, the gauntlet at Vicksburg, the bombardment at Port Hudson, and the Battle of Mobile Bay.

He served as Commandant of Midshipmen at the United States Naval Academy from 1875 to 1878 — the chief discipline and training officer for the entire midshipman corps. He had previously commanded the screw steamship USS Saco with the Asiatic Fleet from 1870 to 1872.

narrative_blocks.header: Two USS Terry destroyers were named for him
narrative_blocks.body: Two United States Navy ships have been named USS Terry in his honor — the destroyer DD-25 (commissioned 1910, served WWI) and a later destroyer DD-513. He died of tuberculosis at Manitou Springs in 1882 and is buried at the U.S. Naval Academy Cemetery.

cross_connections.display_label: First cousin; both Civil War officers leading the charge
Both fought at Mobile Bay under Admiral Farragut, 1864

military_service.unit: USS Richmond, Western Gulf Blockading Squadron
battles: Battle of the Head of Passes (1861)
military_service.notes: Career US Navy officer 1853-1881. Naval Academy Commandant of Midshipmen 1875-1878.

burial.plot_notes: United States Naval Academy Cemetery, Annapolis MD. Granite stone column over seven feet tall, made to look as if broken off at jagged angle. Inscription: 'Commander Edward Terry who fought valiantly at the battles of Mobile Bay, Vicksburg, and New Orleans.'

notable.notable_blurb: Civil War Brig General; US Congressman from Michigan

parents.father_research_notes: Robert Stuart (1785-1848), fur trader; key figure in John Jacob Astor's Pacific Fur Company; led the 1812-13 overland Astoria expedition that established the Astoria fur post on the Columbia River.

career.notes: Admitted to bar 1842. City attorney for Detroit; prosecuting attorney for Wayne County 1844.

Democrat. Chaired Committee on Expenditures in the Department of the Treasury. Defeated for re-election (1907 Michigan Pioneer & Historical Society work attributed loss to 'changing tide of Michigan politics'; 1860 NYT cited Kansas-Nebraska Act stance).
Befriended Stephen Douglas. Reputation ruined by 1860 divorce-case scandal involving banker Isaac Burch.

military_service.unit: 42nd Illinois Infantry then 55th Illinois Infantry; brigade then division command in Sherman's and McClernand's commands
military_service.wound_notes: Shoulder wound at Battle of Shiloh, April 6, 1862.
military_service.notes: Raised both 42nd and 55th Illinois Infantry Regiments at his own expense. Provisionally appointed Brig. Gen. Nov 29, 1862; promotion REJECTED by US Senate Mar 11, 1863 (reason never published). Sherman removed him from command. Resigned Apr 3, 1863.

narrative_blocks.header: Wounded in the shoulder at Battle of Shiloh
narrative_blocks.body: Commanding a brigade in Sherman's division on April 6, 1862, his men were attacked at dawn in their isolated camp near Pittsburg Landing. He rallied two of his three regiments before taking a shoulder wound and turning over command to Colonel Thomas Kilby Smith.

narrative_blocks.header: Father was Astoria expedition fur trader Robert Stuart
narrative_blocks.body: His father Robert Stuart was a key figure in John Jacob Astor's Pacific Fur Company — leader of the 1812-13 overland expedition that established the Astoria fur post on the Columbia River. He inherited the family appetite for distance and risk, moving West to Detroit before the war.

narrative_blocks.header: Senate rejected his Brigadier General promotion 1863
narrative_blocks.body: Provisionally appointed Brig. Gen. November 29, 1862, his promotion was rejected by the US Senate on March 11, 1863, for reasons that were never published. Sherman removed him from command and he resigned April 3, 1863.

As a Chicago attorney for the Illinois Central, his reputation was destroyed when banker Isaac Burch accused his wife Mary of adultery with him in 1860. The Illinois governor refused to let him raise Civil War regiments; he appealed directly to the federal government and equipped two regiments with his own funds.

narrative_blocks.header: She raised her grandsons after their mother died
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Benson married Brigadier General David Stuart, who led a regiment in Sherman's division at the Battle of Shiloh. Widowed, she moved to Annapolis to raise her grandsons after their own mother died, and a house there long carried her name.

INST114.name: United States Naval Academy
INST114.notes: Founded 1845 at Annapolis Maryland. Hooker-tree connections through Adm. George Dewey (TD0141, Acting Midshipman September 23, 1854, graduated June 18, 1858 fifth of fourteen; instructor 1868-1870; commanded USS Constitution as training ship 1867-1870; laid Memorial Chapel cornerstone June 1904) and his nephew Cdr. Theodore Gibbs Dewey (TD0150, graduate). Dewey Hall and Dewey Field at the Academy named for the Admiral. Significant beneficiary of George Goodwin Dewey's 1963 estate.
